The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

revision.comment: removed [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]; added [[Category:Completion tier 4]] using [[Help:Gadget-HotCat|HotCat]]
revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine—>[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tokoyo-Hime Shrine—>[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|牛頭天王}}" to "|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}}"

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]

Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]
