revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}.== See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also! == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|牛頭天王" to "|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō"
revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year }} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year }}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:New]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year }} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year }}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門}} [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:New]]

(No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|12=draft}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=draft}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王|12=draft}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|12=draft}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市|12=draft}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|12=draft}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=draft}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=draft}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部|12=draft}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=draft}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=draft}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=draft}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year|12=draft}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=draft}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year|12=draft}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=draft}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=draft}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=draft}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=draft}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=draft}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|12=draft}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]]

Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市|12=draft}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|12=draft}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=draft}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=draft}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部|12=draft}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=draft}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=draft}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=draft}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year|12=draft}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=draft}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year|12=draft}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=draft}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=draft}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=draft}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=draft}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=draft}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|12=draft}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]]

Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=draft|12=draft}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部|12=draft|12=draft}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=draft|12=draft}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft|12=draft}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=draft|12=draft}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year|12=draft|12=draft}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft|12=draft}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft|12=draft}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=draft|12=draft}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year|12=draft|12=draft}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft|12=draft}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft|12=draft}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=draft|12=draft}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=draft|12=draft}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|12=draft|12=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|12=draft|12=draft}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市|12=draft|12=draft}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|12=draft|12=draft}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]

Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|12=draft|12=draft}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|12=draft|12=draft}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=draft|12=draft}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部|12=draft|12=draft}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=draft|12=draft}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft|12=draft}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=draft|12=draft}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year|12=draft|12=draft}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft|12=draft}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft|12=draft}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=draft|12=draft}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year|12=draft|12=draft}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft|12=draft}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft|12=draft}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=draft|12=draft}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=draft|12=draft}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|12=draft|12=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|12=draft|12=draft}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}}. revision.comment: 24 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:常世岐姫神社 up to rev 93025687
revision.text:
'''常世岐姫神社'''（とこよきひめじんじゃ）は、[[燕王]][[公孫淵]]を祖と称する[[常世氏]]が信奉する神（'''常世岐姫神'''）を祀る[[神社]]であり、[[日本]]全国に数社ある。 [[大阪府]][[八尾市]]神宮寺にある神社が[[本宮]]であるとされ、残り（分社）は[[埼玉県]]内に確認できる。特に[[行田市]][[荒木 (行田市)|荒木]]に所在する神社は「荒木」を冠して「[[荒木常世岐姫神社]]」と称する場合がある。[[深谷市]][[樫合]]にも所在する。 本項では大阪府八尾市にある常世岐姫神社について説明する。 ---- {{神社 |名称 = 常世岐姫神社 |画像 = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = 大阪府八尾市神宮寺5-173 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |祭神 = 常世岐姫命 |社格 = 式内小社、旧村社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = 八王子神社 |札所等 = |例祭 = 10月23日（秋祭） |神事 = }} 大阪府八尾市[[神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|神宮寺]]にある'''常世岐姫神社'''は、『[[延喜式神名帳]]』に記される[[河内国]][[大県郡]][[神宮寺村]]の[[式内社]]である。 常世岐姫神社の正式名称は[[明治時代]]以降のものであり、それまでは「'''[[八王子神社]]'''（はちおうじじんじゃ）」と称していた。現在も正式名称よりも旧名称のほうが知られており、地図や看板・社頭の石標・八尾市教育委員会の説明標にも八王子神社と記されている。 == 祭神 == '''[[常世岐姫命]]'''を祀る。 祭神は当地に蟠踞した常世氏がその祖神を祀ったものと考えられるが、現在は[[出産|安産]]の神として知られている。 == 歴史 == 当地は古代に帰化したと思われる染色技術者集団（[[品部]]）の[[赤染部]]（あかそめべ）の本拠地で、赤染とは[[アカネ色素|茜染め]]のことであるが、[[赤染氏]]が[[伴造]]としてこれらを管理していたと考えられ吉田実「常世岐姫神社」（『日本の神々』所収）。、時代は降って[[鎌倉時代]]にも当地の人々が[[鎌倉幕府|幕府]]から「河内国藍御作手（あいみつくて）奉行」に任じられて染色技術を諸国で指導したという（『[[吾妻鑑]]』）。 伴造の赤染氏（[[カバネ|姓（かばね）]]は[[造|造姓]]）の一族には[[壬申の乱]]で活躍した者もおり（『[[日本書紀]]』）、当地についてみれば、[[宝亀]]8年（[[777年]]）の夏に正六位上の赤染人足ら13人が常世連（とこよのむらじ）を賜姓されたという記録があり『[[続日本紀]]』宝亀8年4月乙未（14日）条。、『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の河内国[[諸蕃]]に「常世連。[[燕王|燕の国王]]、[[公孫淵]]の後なり」と記されており、当地を燕の[[公孫氏 (遼東)|公孫氏]]の流れを汲むと称する帰化人が[[本貫]]としていた事が判り、またその子孫が茜染めをしていたとも伝わっているので、赤染氏（常世氏）が祖神を祀ったものと推定できる。 神社が創建された年代は不詳であるが、[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]9年（[[867年]]）官社に預かり『[[日本三代実録]]』同年2月26日条。、延喜の制で小社（官幣小社）に列した。 近世には、神宮寺村は北隣の[[高安郡]][[恩智村]]とともに[[恩智神社]]の氏地であった。恩智神社は中世末に[[牛頭天王]]を[[勧請]]しており、それに関連して当社が「八王子社」といわれており（八王子は牛頭天王の8柱の御子神）、八王子神社と呼ばれる所以である。『[[河内名所図会]]』にも、「今、八王子と称する」と記されていた。 == 交通 == * [[近鉄大阪線]][[恩智駅]]から東へ約500m進み、[[国道170号]]線旧道（[[東高野街道]]）を南へさらに約800m進む。さらに山側へ約50m進む。（駐車場無し） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 谷川健一編『日本の神々―神社と聖地』第3巻（摂津・河内・和泉・淡路）《新装復刊》、白水社、2000年、{{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}} == 関連項目 == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|大阪府八尾市の八王子神社}} * [[赤染徳足]] * [[赤染衛門]] {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とこよきひめしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:渡来人|社とこよきひめしんしや]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:赤染氏|社とこよきひめしんしや]] [[Category:八尾市の建築物]] [[Category:八尾市の歴史]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[常世岐姫神社]] to [[Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|qq=draft|qq=draft}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|qq=draft|qq=draft}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Akasome Emon|ja|赤染衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigrants to Japan]] [[Category:Tokoyo Clan]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|ja|公孫淵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|King of Yan|ja|燕王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|ja|八尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda, Saitama|ja|行田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
