The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:八尾市の建築物]] [[Category:八尾市の歴史]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. '''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:八尾市の建築物]] [[Category:八尾市の歴史]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:渡来人]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Akasome Clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:渡来人]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:渡来人]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:渡来人]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category History of Yao, Osaka Prefecture
revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:渡来人]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:渡来人]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]

Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. '''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyokihime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*). During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡}}. The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]). A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|qq=}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyokihime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji." == Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|qq=}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|qq=}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]] {{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Tokoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[sn:okoyo-Hime Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:常世氏]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:赤染氏]]

revision.text:
'''Tokoyokihime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|qq=}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|qq=}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyokihime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]]. This article focuses on Tokoyokihime Shrine in Yao, Osaka. ---- {{Shrine |name = Tokoyokihime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hachioji-jinja (Yao), torii.jpg|280px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) }} Tokoyokihime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. The formal name "Tokoyokihime Shrine" dates from the [[Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education. == Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''. It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産}}. == History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|qq=}}. A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day. This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|qq=}} who had immigrated to Japan. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day. The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown.
