<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11481301]] Wikidata name: Tokoyokihime-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokoyoki-hime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|1=Tokoyo clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109599602}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|1=Jingūji, Yao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589472}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyoki-hime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]].
This article focuses on Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in Yao, Osaka.
---- {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tokoyoki-hime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hachioji-Shrine, Yao01.JPG |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) |coordinates = {{Coord|34.601611|N|135.632444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]].
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[:en:Onji Station|Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)

The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[:en:Onji Station|Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|1=Akasome Tokotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635734}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11481301]] Wikidata name: Tokoyokihime-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokoyoki-hime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|1=Tokoyo clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109599602}}, who claim descent from [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], the [[:en:Prince of Yan|King of Yan]]. Several such shrines exist across [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|1=Jingūji, Yao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589472}}, [[:en:Yao, Osaka|Yao, Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda, Saitama]], is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyoki-hime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]].
This article focuses on Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in Yao, Osaka.
---- {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tokoyoki-hime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hachioji-Shrine, Yao01.JPG |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) |coordinates = {{Coord|34.601611|N|135.632444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]].
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[:en:Onji Station|Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)

Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]].
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] (as recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of [[:en:Gongsun Yuan|Gongsun Yuan]], King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『[[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s [[:en:Onji Station|Onji Station]], head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|1=Akasome Tokotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635734}} * [[:en:Akazome Emon|Akasome Emon]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11481301]] Wikidata name: Tokoyokihime-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokoyoki-hime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|1=Tokoyo clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109599602}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, the {{ill|Prince of Yan|en|Prince of Yan|WD=Q5187291|lt=King of Yan}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|1=Jingūji, Yao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589472}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|WD=Q490872|lt=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda, Saitama}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyoki-hime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}.
This article focuses on Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in Yao, Osaka.
---- {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tokoyoki-hime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hachioji-Shrine, Yao01.JPG |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) |coordinates = {{Coord|34.601611|N|135.632444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}.
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s {{ill|Onji Station|en|Onji Station|WD=Q7093974|lt=Onji Station}}, head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|1=Jingūji, Yao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589472}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|WD=Q490872|lt=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda, Saitama}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyoki-hime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}.
This article focuses on Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in Yao, Osaka.
---- {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tokoyoki-hime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hachioji-Shrine, Yao01.JPG |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) |coordinates = {{Coord|34.601611|N|135.632444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}.
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s {{ill|Onji Station|en|Onji Station|WD=Q7093974|lt=Onji Station}}, head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)

The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11481301]] Wikidata name: Tokoyokihime-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokoyoki-hime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|1=Tokoyo clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109599602}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, the {{ill|Prince of Yan|en|Prince of Yan|WD=Q5187291|lt=King of Yan}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|1=Jingūji, Yao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589472}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|WD=Q490872|lt=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda, Saitama}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyoki-hime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}.
This article focuses on Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in Yao, Osaka.
---- {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tokoyoki-hime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hachioji-Shrine, Yao01.JPG |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) |coordinates = {{Coord|34.601611|N|135.632444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}.
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s {{ill|Onji Station|en|Onji Station|WD=Q7093974|lt=Onji Station}}, head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|1=Akasome Tokotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635734}} * {{ill|Akazome Emon|en|Akazome Emon|WD=Q269755|lt=Akasome Emon}}
{{translated page|ja|常世岐姫神社|version=93025687|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] [[ja:%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%E5%B2%90%E5%A7%AB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]]

However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s {{ill|Onji Station|en|Onji Station|WD=Q7093974|lt=Onji Station}}, head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:常世岐姫神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11481301]] Wikidata name: Tokoyokihime-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokoyoki-hime Shrine''' (常世岐姫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} that enshrines the deity '''Tokoyokihime-no-Kami''' (常世岐姫神), worshipped by the {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo clan|1=Tokoyo clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109599602}}, who claim descent from {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, the {{ill|Prince of Yan|en|Prince of Yan|WD=Q5187291|lt=King of Yan}}. Several such shrines exist across {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
The shrine located in {{ill|Jingūji, Yao|ja|神宮寺 (八尾市の地名)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingūji, Yao|1=Jingūji, Yao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589472}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|WD=Q490872|lt=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, is regarded as the '''main shrine''' (本宮), while others (branch shrines) can be found in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. Notably, the shrine in {{ill|Araki, Gyōda|ja|荒木 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki, Gyōda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda, Saitama}}, is sometimes called "'''Araki Tokoyoki-hime Shrine'''" (荒木常世岐姫神社). Another branch shrine exists in {{ill|Kashiai, Fukaya|ja|樫合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiai, Fukaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}.
This article focuses on Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in Yao, Osaka.
---- {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tokoyoki-hime Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hachioji-Shrine, Yao01.JPG |location = 5-173 Jingūji, Yao, Osaka Prefecture |coords = {{coord|34|36|05.8|N|135|37|56.8|E|scale:20000}} |enshrined_deities = Tokoyokihime no Mikoto |shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (small shrine), Former Village Shrine |established = Unknown |main_hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] |alias = Hachioji Shrine |annual_festival = October 23 (Autumn Festival) |coordinates = {{Coord|34.601611|N|135.632444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
Tokoyoki-hime Shrine in [[Jingūji (Yao)|Jingūji]], Yao, Osaka Prefecture, is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as a shrine of the [[Ōagata District]] (大県郡), {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}.
The formal name "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" dates from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}. Previously, it was called "'''Hachioji Shrine'''" (八王子神社). Even today, the former name is more commonly recognized, as seen on maps, signboards, shrine markers, and information boards by the Yao City Board of Education.
== Enshrined deity == The shrine enshrines '''{{ill|Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja|常世岐姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyokihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''.
It is believed that the Tokoyo clan, who settled in this area, enshrined their ancestral deity here. In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s {{ill|Onji Station|en|Onji Station|WD=Q7093974|lt=Onji Station}}, head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)

In modern times, the shrine is particularly known as a deity of {{ill|safe childbirth|ja|安産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safe childbirth|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 出産|1=birth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q14819852}}.
== History == This area was historically the homeland of the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a group of skilled dye artisans ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) specializing in {{ill|madder dyeing|ja|アカネ色素|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/madder dyeing|1=Madder color|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11282714}}. The clan was led by the {{ill|Akazome clan|ja|赤染氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazome clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who acted as {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomonomiyatsuko|lt=Tomo no miyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no miyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} overseeing the dye industry.<ref>Yoshida Minoru, "Tokoyoki-hime Shrine" (from *Nihon no Kamigami*).</ref> During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the local people were appointed as "藍御作手奉行" (Aimitsukute Bugyō), officials responsible for instructing other regions in dyeing techniques, as recorded in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吾妻鏡|1=Azuma Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042324}}.
The Akazome clan (who held the {{ill|Zō kabane|ja|造姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zō kabane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title) produced several notable figures, including those involved in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}).
A historical record from the eighth month of {{ill|Hōki 8 (777)|ja|宝亀8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki 8 (777)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} mentions that 13 people from the Akazome clan were granted the surname '''Tokoyo no Muraji''' (常世連).<ref>『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}』, Hōki 8, 4th month, 14th day.</ref> Additionally, the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} lists the Tokoyo clan in Kawachi Province as "descendants of {{ill|Gongsun Yuan|en|Gongsun Yuan|WD=Q735405|lt=Gongsun Yuan}}, King of Yan" (燕の国王、公孫淵の後なり). This suggests that the area was a homeland for descendants of the {{ill|Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|ja|公孫氏 (遼東)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongsun clan (Liaodong)|1=Gongsun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391985}} who had immigrated to Japan. They are also believed to have engaged in madder dyeing, reinforcing the connection between the Akazome (Tokoyo) clan and the shrine's founding.
The exact date of the shrine’s establishment is unknown. However, in {{ill|Jōgan 9 (867)|ja|貞観9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 9 (867)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was recognized as a government-sponsored shrine.<ref>『{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}』, entry for Jōgan 9, 2nd month, 26th day.</ref> During the {{ill|Engi era|ja|延喜時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was classified as a '''Shōsha''' (官幣小社).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Jingūji village, along with neighboring {{ill|Onchi Village|ja|恩智村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Takayasu District|ja|高安郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu District|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高安 (八尾市)|1=Takayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669131}}, fell under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492258}}. Since Onchi Shrine had enshrined the deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} in the late medieval period, Tokoyoki-hime Shrine became associated with the Eight Princes (八王子), the children of Gozu Tennō, leading to its alternate name '''Hachioji Shrine'''. This naming is confirmed in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that "it is now called Hachioji."
== Access == * From [[:en:Kintetsu Osaka Line|Kintetsu Osaka Line]]'s {{ill|Onji Station|en|Onji Station|WD=Q7093974|lt=Onji Station}}, head east approximately 500 meters, then proceed south along the old route of [[:en:National Route 170|National Route 170]] ({{ill|Higashi Kōya Kaidō|ja|東高野街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kōya Kaidō|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528777}}) for another 800 meters. The shrine is about 50 meters further uphill. (No parking available.)
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * Tanikawa Kenichi (ed.), *Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places*, Vol. 3 (Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji), Hakusuisha, 2000, {{ISBN2|978-4-560-02503-1}}. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:Hachioji-Shrine,_Yao|Hachioji Shrine in Yao, Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Akasome Tokotari|ja|赤染徳足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasome Tokotari|1=Akasome Tokotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635734}} * {{ill|Akazome Emon|en|Akazome Emon|WD=Q269755|lt=Akasome Emon}}
